lines.text: Chapter five, section five. Test yourself.
For all of the following problems, assume you are playing no-limit hold 'em at a nine-handed table with two-dollar and five-dollar blinds and five-hundred-dollar effective stacks.
Problem one.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Ace of diamonds and Jack of diamonds.
The big blind calls.
Your read on this player is that he is a typical splashy recreational player: he gets involved in too many pots before the flop but becomes tight and passive when large bets are required.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades, and the big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
The answer is to bet.
Here is the reasoning.

At equilibrium, you would likely be roughly indifferent in this spot — betting your top pair with a good kicker is tempting because you want value, but checking is also reasonable because an Ace-high board hits many opponent ranges and betting exposes you to a check-raise from a balanced, polarized range that could put you in a genuinely difficult position.
For a hand as strong as Ace-Jack but not strong enough to comfortably withstand a well-constructed check-raise, the equilibrium treatment might be a mix.
But that indifference disappears as soon as you factor in your read.

A splashy recreational player calls too many hands before the flop, which means his range connecting with this Ace-high board is loaded with dominated Aces — Ace-rag combinations he would not have called with at equilibrium — and draws he picked up with weak suited or connected hands.
Both of those categories are exactly what you want to extract value from with Ace-Jack.
Critically, your read also says this player is tight and passive when it comes to putting in big bets.
That means the thing that makes betting scary at equilibrium — the check-raise — is essentially off the table.
One of the main reasons you might check a strong but not unbeatable hand on a dangerous board is the risk of getting raised off your equity by a polarized range.
Against a player who never check-raises, that fear evaporates.
Betting is strictly the best play.
Problem two.
You bet twenty dollars with your Ace-Jack and the big blind calls.
The turn is the Eight of spades, completing a possible flush on a board of Ace, Nine, Six, Eight, all with three spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
This is a legitimately close decision even with your reads in hand.
You still hold top pair with a good kicker, and there are hands in his range that you either want to charge for their draws or fold out of their equity.
But the Eight of spades is a threatening card.
Many hands that call your flop bet could now have a flush, and several others have picked up additional straight draws.
Against any player who checks, there is a real possibility that you are now behind and drawing very thin.

The more confident you are that your read holds on the turn — specifically that this player will not raise unless he has a hand that crushes you — the more comfortable you can be with betting and folding if raised.
Checking is also perfectly fine here.

The key takeaway for the four-step process is that reads do not always produce a definitive answer; sometimes they help you understand why a decision is genuinely close and that checking is defensible rather than exploitable.
Problem three.
You check the turn, and the river comes the Three of diamonds, making the final board Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades, Eight of spades, Three of diamonds.
The big blind bets sixty dollars, just under the size of the pot.
What is your play?
Fold.
The first step in working through this spot is to recognize what your hand actually is at this point.
Ace-Jack on a four-spade board where you do not hold a spade is a bluff-catcher.
Your read told you that this player does not make big value bets with worse Aces.
That means when he fires sixty dollars into a pot on the river, he either has a hand that beats you — a flush, a straight, two pair or better — or he is bluffing.
The question is not whether he could be bluffing; he certainly could be.
The question is whether he bluffs at a high enough frequency relative to his value hands to make calling profitable.

As we established in our game theory work, a bluff-catcher facing a bet from a polarized range is roughly indifferent at equilibrium — if your opponent bluffs at exactly the right frequency, calling and folding produce the same EV.
So the relevant question here is not just can he be bluffing but rather does he bluff out of proportion to the number of value hands he can hold.
This board has created a large number of possible value hands for the big blind — any spade gave him a flush, any seven gave him a straight, and he could have two pair multiple ways.
Getting to a point where his bluffing frequency is high enough relative to that value density to make your call profitable would require him to be bluffing a very large fraction of his range.
And your read specifically describes him as passive when big bets are involved.
A tight, passive player who is not inclined to bluff big on the river is almost certainly not bluffing here at a frequency that justifies your call.
While you might be roughly indifferent at equilibrium, this read pushes you firmly toward folding.
Problem four.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the King of spades and Jack of spades.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very loose.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
At first glance, bluffing into a very loose opponent on an Ace-high board might seem like a poor choice — loose players call too much, so your bluffs will face more resistance.
But look carefully at what you are holding.
King-Jack of spades on Ace-Nine-Six with two spades is not really a pure bluff.
You have two overcards that could improve to strong hands, and you have a flush draw that will frequently get there by the river.
Against a loose player's calling range, you are ahead of a significant portion of the hands he might call with.
A hand like Queen of hearts, Eight of hearts has equity against you, but you would not be unhappy at all folding it out now.
Even though a loose opponent is not the ideal target for stone-cold bluffs, your hand here has enough raw value and equity that betting is correct.
If you are raised, you have a clear fold, and if you are called, you have real outs.
Problem five.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Jack of hearts and Ten of hearts.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very tight and straightforward both before and after the flop — he is afraid to slow-play and never bluffs, and he always assumes that his opponents hit the board.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Check, even though betting would likely be profitable.
Here is why checking is the most profitable way to exploit this particular opponent.
Tight players have strong pre-flop ranges — they fold many of the weak hands before the flop that a splashy player would call with.
That means when they do contest pots, their hands connect with flops more reliably.
A tight player on this Ace-high board has either hit an Ace, picked up a draw, or genuinely missed.
If he has hit an Ace or has a strong draw, your flop bet is not folding him out anyway, so the bet buys you nothing and costs you chips.
If he has completely missed, he was going to fold on any street.
The insight comes from the second half of your read: this player is afraid to bluff.
If you check to him and he checks back, that gives you very strong information that he does not have a hand he plans to fight with.
On the turn, you can then make the same bluff you would have made on the flop, but now you have confirmation that he plans to fold, which makes the bluff much more likely to succeed.
If you check to him and he bets, you can fold confidently — this player does not bluff, so his bet is a real hand, and you save the chips you would have lost by leading the flop into him.
There is a nuance about board texture worth noting here.

On boards where the turn card could significantly change how the opponent feels about his hand — giving him a new pair, completing a draw, or threatening his holding — it can be better to bluff immediately rather than wait.
On this board, though, the dynamics are clear: he either connected with the Ace or he did not, and a turn card is unlikely to change that picture dramatically.
Gathering the free information by checking is the highest-EV play.
Problem six.
You are first to act before the flop and raise to fifteen dollars with the Nine of diamonds and Nine of hearts.
The big blind calls.
Your read is that this player is very tight and straightforward — afraid to slow-play, never bluffs, and always assumes his opponents hit the board.
The flop comes Ace of hearts, Nine of spades, Six of spades.
The big blind checks.
Do you bet or check?
Bet.
This hand looks superficially similar to the previous one — same opponent read, same board — but the hand itself changes everything.
You have just flopped a set of Nines.
This is not a bluffing situation at all; it is a value situation, and that completely alters the calculus.
Against a tight, straightforward opponent who will not slow-play, your set of Nines is almost certainly the best hand right now.
If he has an Ace, he is going to give you action — and you want that action right now, before the board gets more threatening and he starts second-guessing his Ace.
A fourth spade, a straight-completing Ten or Seven, or any other scary card could cause even a tight player to pump the brakes with top pair.
Getting the money in on the flop when you are a big favorite is correct.
If he has a draw, you want to charge him for it.
If he has nothing, he is not bluffing at you on the turn anyway, so checking does not create additional profit — he simply will not put chips in without a reason to.
The lesson from problems five and six side by side is that your read about an opponent is only half the equation.
The other half is what you are holding.
The same read — tight, passive, non-bluffing player — produces completely different optimal strategies depending on whether you have a bluffing candidate or a strong value hand.
Systematically working through the four-step process for each hand type is what keeps you from misapplying a read that is otherwise accurate.
